point_001: The novelty of the approach is limited, as incorporating entity type information (analogous to POS tagging or named entity typing) is a well-established practice in NLP pipelines, making the technical contribution incremental.
point_002: The proposed methods yield significant improvements only on the synthetic CLUTRR dataset but fail to produce meaningful gains on real-world QA datasets (HotpotQA, CoQA), undermining the practical utility of the approach.
point_003: The paper lacks deeper analysis explaining why entity abstraction helps on synthetic data but not on natural-language datasets—e.g., whether the failure is due to noisy entity type predictions, task characteristics, or model capacity.
point_004: Experiments are conducted exclusively with T5-small, a relatively small model; findings may not generalize to larger, stronger pretrained language models that are more commonly used in practice.
point_005: The baseline performance on the QA datasets is already far below current state-of-the-art, making it impossible to determine whether the proposed methods would remain beneficial when applied to stronger baselines.
point_006: No guidance is provided on which of the five architectural variants to choose for a new reasoning task, since different variants work best on different datasets, making the method impractical without exhaustive search.
point_007: There are inconsistencies and unclear statements in the paper: (a) the total dataset size (390,000 examples split 70/30) conflicts with the reported test set size of 10,000; (b) the meaning of 'accept both' for inverse relations (son/daughter) is ambiguous; (c) the CLUTRR appendix example does not follow the answer template described in the text; (d) TACRED is mentioned in the appendix without explanation of how it is used.
point_008: The paper does not include ablation studies or analytic experiments that would clarify how and why the different model variants behave differently across datasets, limiting the interpretability of the results.
